content.problemStatement:
Chef has been promoted to the rank of General in the Siruseri Armed Forces!

As part of his work, he is to lead qqq military expeditions into the neighbouring Republic of Tutaria.

Tutaria has nnn cities. The cities are numbered from 111 to nnn. These cities are connected by n‚àí1n-1n‚àí1 bidirectional roads, in a way that it is possible to travel from any city to any other city.

In the iiith expedition, Chef travels from city aia_iai‚Äã to city bib_ibi‚Äã in Tutaria. To do this, he will take a route, such that he never visits any city more than one time. More formally, Chef's expedition can be represented as a sequence sss of mmm distinct cities, where s1=ais_1 = a_is1‚Äã=ai‚Äã, sm=bis_m = b_ism‚Äã=bi‚Äã, and there is a road between sjs_jsj‚Äã and sj+1s_{j+1}sj+1‚Äã for all 1‚â§j<m1 \leq j < m1‚â§j<m.

Each city in Tutaria also has gold to loot. During an expedition, for each city Chef visits, he can choose whether he would or would not like to loot the city. Looting city iii will give Chef viv_ivi‚Äã gold ingots. However, since he wants to avoid causing too much discontentment, he will not loot two adjacent cities during the same expedition. Notice that all expeditions are independent of each other - his choice of cities to loot during one expedition does not affect his options in subsequent expeditions.

Subject to these requirements, find the highest total gold that Chef can receive on each trip.


Input Format
The first line of input will contain two space separated integers, nnn and qqq. nnn represents the number of cities in Tutaria, while qqq represents the number of expeditions Chef will make.


The second line of input contains nnn space-separated integers, representing v1,v2...vnv_1, v_2 ... v_nv1‚Äã,v2‚Äã...vn‚Äã.


The following n‚àí1n-1n‚àí1 lines of input each contain two space separated integers. The iiith of these lines contains xix_ixi‚Äã and yiy_iyi‚Äã, representing a road between xix_ixi‚Äã and yiy_iyi‚Äã.


The final qqq lines of input each contain two space-separated integers. The iiith of these lines contains aia_iai‚Äã and bib_ibi‚Äã, representing an expedition Chef will make from city aia_iai‚Äã to city bib_ibi‚Äã.



Output Format
Output qqq lines, each containing one integer. The integer on the iiith line should be the maximum possible total gold that can be gained in the iiith expedition.
constraints: The input is divided into multiple subtasks. You will get the points allocated for a subtask if and only if you solve every testcase in the subtask correctly, within the time limit.

Please remember that when you make a submission to a problem, we will automatically consider your submission for every single subtask, and also the full problem. After the contest, your scores will be 'stitched'. This means that if you solve a subtask in any of your submissions, you will get the points for the subtask in your final score. There is no need to combine your solutions for different subtasks into the same submission.
For all subtasks:

1‚â§q‚â§5√ó1051 \leq q \leq 5 \times 10^51‚â§q‚â§5√ó105
0‚â§vi‚â§1090 \leq v_i \leq 10^90‚â§vi‚Äã‚â§109 for all 1‚â§i‚â§n1 \leq i \leq n1‚â§i‚â§n
1‚â§xi,yi‚â§n1 \leq x_i, y_i \leq n1‚â§xi‚Äã,yi‚Äã‚â§n and xi‚âÝyix_i \neq y_ixi‚ÄãÓÄÝ=yi‚Äã for all 1‚â§i‚â§n‚àí11 \leq i \leq n-11‚â§i‚â§n‚àí1
1‚â§ai,bi‚â§n1 \leq a_i, b_i \leq n1‚â§ai‚Äã,bi‚Äã‚â§n for all 1‚â§i‚â§q1 \leq i \leq q1‚â§i‚â§q
Subtasks
We define the Line constraint as the following set of conditions:
xi=ix_i = ixi‚Äã=i, yi=i+1y_i = i+1yi‚Äã=i+1 for all 1‚â§i‚â§n‚àí11 \leq i \leq n-11‚â§i‚â§n‚àí1
ai‚â§bia_i \leq b_iai‚Äã‚â§bi‚Äã for all 1‚â§i‚â§q1 \leq i \leq q1‚â§i‚â§q
These are the subtasks for the problem:
Subtask 1 [4 points]: n=1n=1n=1 and q=1q=1q=1
Subtask 2 [6 points]: n,q‚â§2000n, q \leq 2000n,q‚â§2000, Line, vi=1v_i = 1vi‚Äã=1
Subtask 3 [10 points]: n,q‚â§2000n, q \leq 2000n,q‚â§2000, Line, vi‚â•vi+1v_i \geq v_{i+1}vi‚Äã‚â•vi+1‚Äã
Subtask 4 [12 points]: n,q‚â§2000n, q \leq 2000n,q‚â§2000, Line
Subtask 5 [8 points]: q‚â§5000q \leq 5000q‚â§5000, Line, and at most 10 values of viv_ivi‚Äã are non zero.
Subtask 6 [14 points]: Line
Subtask 7 [9 points]: n,q‚â§2000n, q \leq 2000n,q‚â§2000
Subtask 8 [7 points]: ai=1a_i = 1ai‚Äã=1 for all queries.
Subtask 9 [12 points]: City 111 is along the simple path from aia_iai‚Äã to bib_ibi‚Äã for all trips.
Subtask 10 [18 points]: No additional constraints.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Sample Input #1 is valid for subtasks 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10.
In this sample, Chef can pick any subset of cities to loot along the path from city 1 to city 1, itself. So he has two options: either to loot the cities at city 1 (giving him 444 gold), or to not loot any cities at all (giving him 000 gold). Since he is trying to maximise his gold, he chooses to loot the city, for an answer of 444 gold.

Explanation:
Sample Input #2 is valid for subtasks 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 and 10.
All cities in this sample have 1 gold.
In the first trip, Chef is travelling from city 1 to 3. He can choose to loot city 1 and 3, for a total of 2 gold.
In the second trip, Chef is travelling from city 1 to 6. He can choose to loot city 1, 4 and 6, for a total of 3 gold.
In the third trip, Chef is travelling from city 1 to 2. He can loot either city 1 or city 2 (not both!), and get 1 gold.
In the fourth trip, Chef is travelling from city 3 to city 6. He can choose to loot city 4 and 6 for a total of 2 gold.
Do note that in many of these trips, Chef could have chosen to loot a different subset of cities and obtained the same gold. Only one example per trip is provided.

Explanation:
Sample Input #3 is valid for subtasks 4, 5, 6, 7 and 10.
For the first trip, Chef is travelling from city 4 to city 8. It is optimal for him to loot city 5 and city 8, for a total of 6+3=96 + 3 = 96+3=9 gold.
For the second trip, Chef is travelling from city 9 to city 9. It is optimal for him to loot city 9, for a total of 2 gold.
For the third trip, Chef is travelling from city 8 to city 9. It is optimal for him to loot city 8, for a total of 3 gold.
For the final trip, Chef is travelling from city 1 to city 10. It is optimal for him to loot cities 1, 3, 5, 8 and 10, for a total of 1+6+6+3+1=171 + 6 + 6 + 3 + 1 = 171+6+6+3+1=17 gold.

Explanation:
Sample Input #4 is valid for subtasks 7, 9 and 10.

Explanation:
Sample Input #5 is valid for subtasks 7 and 10.

solution.approach:
To solve the problem, the logic is based on finding the path between the two cities Chef visits and ensuring that the maximum gold can be looted along this path without looting two adjacent cities. The tree structure (with n-1 roads) allows us to use techniques like `Depth First Search` (DFS) and `Lowest Common Ancestor` (LCA) to efficiently find and handle paths. We preprocess the tree with DFS to compute `ancestors` and `depths`, which helps in quickly calculating the LCA of any two cities.

Once the LCA is known, the problem reduces to splitting the path into two segments: from each city to the LCA. `Dynamic programming` is used along these paths to calculate the maximum loot Chef can gather, adhering to the rule that no two adjacent cities can be looted. The `DP` state keeps track of whether the current city is looted or skipped and propagates the maximum possible gold to the next step. After evaluating the two segments, the results are combined to get the optimal solution for that query.
